title: Producer Playbook
intro: The course teaches you to engineer Luau systems. This module covers the other half of shipping a successful game: reading live-game data, understanding how Roblox distributes games, and designing for retention. Concepts are distilled from producer practice; each card links to the official Roblox documentation where the platform describes these systems itself.
note: Supplementary module — added on request. Not part of the LuauX server curriculum and kept separate from the verified course content.

topics.title: Reading the Numbers
topics.summary: Every decision about a live game should start from its data. These are the numbers that matter and what they actually tell you.
points: Check stats before and after every change you ship — a change that doesn't move a number is a guess, not an improvement.
A single metric in isolation misleads. High visits with low retention means discovery is working but the game isn't holding anyone.
Watch trends over days and weeks, not hourly noise.

metrics.meaning: Concurrent users — how many players are in the game right now. The headline health number; peaks show your ceiling.
Total sessions ever. Grows forever, so it measures reach, not current health.
The share of new players who come back one, seven, or thirty days later. D1 is the single strongest signal that the first session worked.
How long a typical session lasts. Short sessions usually mean players run out of understandable things to do.
Players leaving in the first minutes (a bounce). Almost always an onboarding or first-impression problem, not a content problem.
How monetization performs relative to your audience size — revenue alone hides whether it comes from many small or few large purchases.

topics.title: How Discovery Works
topics.summary: Roblox decides which games to show players. You cannot trick that system — you can only feed it the signals it rewards.
points: Recommendations favor games whose players stay, return, and engage. Retention and session quality are the input; visibility is the output.
The home page, search, and the charts are different surfaces — a game can rise on one before the others.
Growth speed matters: a game climbing quickly gets tested in front of more players. That's why iteration velocity feeds discovery.
If distribution drops, the fix is almost never a marketing trick — it's improving the underlying engagement numbers.

topics.title: Idea & Market Research
topics.summary: Ideas are abundant; validated demand is rare. Research is how you tell the difference before spending months building.
points: Study the top and trending charts on a regular schedule — not to copy, but to understand what fantasies players are currently paying attention to.
Play top games deliberately, like study sessions: what do you understand in the first 30 seconds, what pulls you back, where does the money sit?
Ask whether the core fantasy has mass appeal. A niche you love is not automatically a market.
Watch what's rising off-platform too — formats and memes often arrive on Roblox after proving themselves elsewhere.

topics.title: Simplicity × Depth
topics.summary: The winning pattern on the platform: one instantly understandable core mechanic that generates endless situations.
points: A new player should understand cause and effect immediately: press this, that happens. If they have to figure the game out, most won't.
Depth should be emergent (collection, optimization, competition, randomness creating variety) — not a pile of extra features.
Much of the audience is very young. 'Easy to consume' is a design requirement, not a compromise.
Complexity is the default failure mode of skilled developers: every feature you add must survive the question 'does a nine-year-old get this in five seconds?'

topics.title: First Session & Platform UX
topics.summary: D1 retention is decided in the first minutes of the first session — on whatever device the player happens to hold.
points: Play your own game, constantly, on a fresh account — broken prompts and confusing first steps are invisible to the person who built them.
Test on mobile and console, not just desktop. Console and mobile players can be a third or more of your audience, and their input works differently.
Treat the tutorial as a live system: measure, rewrite, and re-test it repeatedly rather than shipping it once.
A short scripted intro (even a simple cutscene) can measurably cut early quits by giving new players context before they're dropped into the world.
Hunt friction relentlessly: every extra tap, unclear button, or dead moment in minute one costs real retention.

topics.title: Retention Loops & Social Flex
topics.summary: Players need a concrete reason to come back tomorrow — and a way to show others what they've built or earned.
points: Design an explicit answer to 'what do I come back for tomorrow?' — a new unlock, a next collection tier, progress that continues.
Curiosity is a loop: 'what's the next thing I could get?' outperforms raw reward size.
Most top games have a social flex element: progress that other players can see, compare, and envy inside the game.
Competition between players (stealing, raiding, rankings) turns single-player progress into a reason to defend and return.

topics.title: Clippability
topics.summary: Moments worth recording are free marketing. Design them on purpose.
points: Watch what creators actually clip from games like yours: dramatic wins, funny physics, absurd fails, big steals.
Exaggerate feedback on your game's peak moments — a ragdoll flying into the void is a clip; a number ticking up is not.
If content creators can make an entertaining video without being paid, your game markets itself.
Ask of every major mechanic: what does its best moment look like in a 20-second clip?

topics.title: Iteration Velocity
topics.summary: The loop that compounds: research → hypothesis → ship → measure → repeat. Speed through this loop is a competitive weapon.
points: Ship small changes frequently — daily patches and substantial weekly updates keep both players and the discovery system engaged.
Every change is an experiment: know beforehand which number it should move, then check whether it did.
Cross-reference multiple sources — your analytics, in-game behavior, community feedback, and competitor data — before trusting a conclusion.
Most attempts won't work. Volume of shipped experiments, not perfection per attempt, is what finds the wins.

topics.title: Team & Production
topics.summary: Execution beats ideas, and execution is a people problem: skill, speed, and clear roles.
points: The idea is a fraction of the outcome; the quality and speed of execution is most of it.
Clear roles help: someone owns building the game, someone owns data, monetization, and distribution. Both jobs are full-time mindsets.
Buying templates or outsourcing without understanding the craft teaches you nothing and shows in the product.
Monetization is a design discipline of its own — plan what players pay for and why it feels fair, don't bolt it on at the end.
